coredata.dc:title: Mechanical and electrical response of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, WenWei
Yang, Sheng
Lin, Cheng
Li, YiDing
coredata.dc:description: Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) value. In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values were performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanical behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results indicate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
SOC dependency
Voltage response
Mechanical behaviors

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 145 ( 8) 128 132 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.04.021 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.04.021 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 The Authors. Published by El evier Ltd. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017, 18â€“20 October 2017, Tianjin, China Mechanical and electrical response f cyl ndrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge WenWei Wanga, b, Sheng Yanga, b, *, Cheng Lina, b, YiDing Lia, b a Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 b National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 Abstract Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) value. In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values were performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanical behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results indicate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery; SOC dependency; Voltage response; Mechanical behaviors 1. Introduction In order to address issues associated with the usage and even the depletion of non-renewable energy sources (in particular, fossil fuels), researches worldwide are actively seeking for another clean and renewable energy sources. As renewable energy storage devices, Lithium-ion battery cells have the highest energy and power densities, compared to other rechargeable batteries. These obvious advantages make them be the dominant energy source for lots of modern industrial products, such s portable electronic devices, hybrid electric vehicles, total electric vehicles * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-18010099602 E-mail address: 1034012081@qq.com Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and eer-review under r sponsibility of t e scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017, 18â€“20 October 2017, Tianjin, China Mechanical and electrical response of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge WenWei Wanga, b, Sheng Yanga, b, *, Cheng Lina, b, YiDing Lia, b a Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 b National E gineeri g Labo atory fo Electr Vehicl s, Beijing I stitute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 Abstract Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) valu . In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values wer performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanic l behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results in icate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taki g SOC effect into account. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Select on and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, R newable Energy Integratio with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery; SOC dependency; Voltage response; Mechanical behaviors 1. Introduction In order to address issues associated with the usage and even the depletion of non-renewable energy sources (in particular, fossil fu ls), researches worldwide are actively s eking for another clean and renewable energy sources. As renewable energy storage devices, Lithium-ion battery cells have the highest energy and power densiti s, compar d to other rechargeable batteries. T ese obvious advantages make them b th domin t energy source for l ts of mod rn industrial products, such as portable electronic devices, hybrid lectric vehicl s, tot l ele tric vehicles * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-18010099602 E-mail address: 1034012081@qq.com 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 and robots. As many advanced applications require battery cells with higher energy and power densities, the safety and reliability of the Lithium-ion battery systems have been an increasingly important issue. With the rapid popularization of electric vehicles (EVs), some vehicles may inevitably experience severe crash accidents. Lithium-ion battery would subsequently suffer from the plastic deformation due to foreign objects intrusion/impact on the Lithium-ion battery package during accidents. As the deformation continued, the cusps of the metal foils or the active particles may tear or puncture the separator. Once separator failure, the internal short circuit may be triggered with the contact between negative and positive electrode. Some pioneering researches were carried out to study the mechanical properties of individual components of the battery cells under different conditions. Mechanical properties and failure mechanisms of battery separators play an important role in possible internal short-circuit and long-term electrochemical degradation [1]. Wierzbicki et al used some tests to determine plastic and fracture properties of shell casing and adopted Modified Mohr-Coulomb fracture model to predict crack initiation and propagation of shell casing [2]. Additionally, some tests were used to investigate elastic and hardness properties of active particles under different SOC and measure biaxial modulus of Si anode [3]. In the battery cell level, the overall mechanical properties or failure mechanisms under different loading conditions were also investigated by several authors. Gilaki et al conducted compression tests on stacks of jellyrollâ€™s individual layers and developed a heterogeneous finite model in LS-DYNA to investigate lateral impact on cylindrical Lithium-ion battery cells. The model was consisted of 22 million solid elements and was solved on a cluster using 192 cores and a memory of 3 GB/core and the solution time was 58 h [4]. Sahraei et al obtained the constitutive response of each component of electrode stack and developed a micro mechanical model. The model was subjected to various loading scenarios to understand the sequence of failure in the multi-layer, multi-material structure of Lithium-ion battery cells [5]. In a previous paper, the authors found that overall mechanical properties of the jellyroll are similar to the clay (clay-like) [6]. Lithium-ion battery cells used in most of the literature to research the mechanical properties are discharged at a state of charge (SOC) 0% to avoid thermal runaway. However, recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties [3] and volume [7] of the active particles will change due to SOC value changing. Lithium- ion battery cells undergo continuous discharging cycles during operations with EVs running. Thus, understanding the SOC dependent mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion battery cells is extremely important because the SOC value of battery cells is usually over zero when vehicle crashes occur during driving. In this investigation, the SOC dependent mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion battery cells was revealed based on the clay-like mechanical properties. A finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account. Then, the finite element model was validated through indentation tests with a rigid rod. For electrochemical behaviors, initial short circuit may be directly reflected by the start of voltage drop. During indentation tests, the voltage responses were measured to research the electrochemical failure behaviors of Lithium-ion battery cells with various SOC values. Experimental results indicate that the voltage would increase slightly at first, and the initial short circuit occurred simultaneously with the load reaching its peak value. 2. Experimental program on battery cells Commercial 18650 Lithium-ion cells were used in this study due to their ease of use in the current investigation, manufactured by SONY. WDW-100 10 T Universal Test Machine was used to perform the compressive test, see Fig. 1. Meanwhile, a safety shield made of polymethyl methacrylate was placed within the boundary of the testing machine to provide necessary protection against possible fire and even explosion. These cylindrical cells with various SOC values were experimented under compression between two flat steel plates at a quasi-static rate of 0.5 mm/min. Compressive experiment on the battery cell at each SOC value was repeated at least three times to ensure the accuracy of the measured data. These measured load-displacement curves are shown in Fig. 2. It is obvious that Lithium-ion battery cells will exhibit mechanical hardening mechanism with the increase in SOC values, as depicted in the Fig. 2. WenWei Wang et al. / Energy Procedia 145 (2018) 128â€“132 129 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017, 18â€“20 October 2017, Tianjin, China Mechanical and electrical response of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge WenWei Wanga, b, Sheng Yanga, b, *, Cheng Lina, b, YiDing Lia, b a Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 b National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 Abstract Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) value. In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values were performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanical behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results indicate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery; SOC dependency; Voltage response; Mechanical behaviors 1. Introduction In order to address issues associated with the usage and even the depletion of non-renewable energy sources (in particular, fossil fuels), researches worldwide are actively seeking for another clean and renewable energy sources. As renewable energy storage devices, Lithium-ion battery cells have the highest energy and power densities, compared to other rechargeable batteries. These obvious advantages make them be the dominant energy source for lots of modern industrial products, such as portable electronic devices, hybrid electric vehicles, total electric vehicles * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-18010099602 E-mail address: 1034012081@qq.com Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and eer-review under r sponsibility of t e scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017, 18â€“20 October 2017, Tianjin, China Mechanical and electrical response of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge WenWei Wanga, b, Sheng Yanga, b, *, Cheng Lina, b, YiDing Lia, b a Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 b National E gineeri g Labo atory fo Electr Vehicl s, Beijing I stitute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081 Abstract Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) valu . In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values wer performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanic l behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results in icate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taki g SOC effect into account. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Select on and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, R newable Energy Integratio with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2017. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery; SOC dependency; Voltage response; Mechanical behaviors 1. Introduction In order to address issues associated with the usage and even the depletion of non-renewable energy sources (in particular, fossil fu ls), researches worldwide are actively s eking for another clean and renewable energy sources. As renewable energy storage devices, Lithium-ion battery cells have the highest energy and power densiti s, compar d to other rechargeable batteries. T ese obvious advantages make them b th domin t energy source for l ts of modern industrial products, such as portable electronic devices, hybrid lectric vehicles, total electric vehicles * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-18010099602 E-mail address: 1034012081@qq.com 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 and robots. As many advanced applications require battery cells with higher energy and power densities, the safety and reliability of the Lithium-ion battery systems have been an increasingly important issue. With the rapid popularization of electric vehicles (EVs), some vehicles may inevitably experience severe crash accidents. Lithium-ion battery would subsequently suffer from the plastic deformation due to foreign objects intrusion/impact on the Lithium-ion battery package during accidents. As the deformation continued, the cusps of the metal foils or the active particles may tear or puncture the separator. Once separator failure, the internal short circuit may be triggered with the contact between negative and positive electrode. Some pioneering researches were carried out to study the mechanical properties of individual components of the battery cells under different conditions. Mechanical properties and failure mechanisms of battery separators play an important role in possible internal short-circuit and long-term electrochemical degradation [1]. Wierzbicki et al used some tests to determine plastic and fracture properties of shell casing and adopted Modified Mohr-Coulomb fracture model to predict crack initiation and propagation of shell casing [2]. Additionally, some tests were used to investigate elastic and hardness properties of active particles under different SOC and measure biaxial modulus of Si anode [3]. In the battery cell level, the overall mechanical properties or failure mechanisms under different loading conditions were also investigated by several authors. Gilaki et al conducted compression tests on stacks of jellyrollâ€™s individual layers and developed a heterogeneous finite model in LS-DYNA to investigate lateral impact on cylindrical Lithium-ion battery cells. The model was consisted of 22 million solid elements and was solved on a cluster using 192 cores and a memory of 3 GB/core and the solution time was 58 h [4]. Sahraei et al obtained the constitutive response of each component of electrode stack and developed a micro mechanical model. The model was subjected to various loading scenarios to understand the sequence of failure in the multi-layer, multi-material structure of Lithium-ion battery cells [5]. In a previous paper, the authors found that overall mechanical properties of the jellyroll are similar to the clay (clay-like) [6]. Lithium-ion battery cells used in most of the literature to research the mechanical properties are discharged at a state of charge (SOC) 0% to avoid thermal runaway. However, recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties [3] and volume [7] of the active particles will change due to SOC value changing. Lithium- ion battery cells undergo continuous discharging cycles during operations with EVs running. Thus, understanding the SOC dependent mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion battery cells is extremely important because the SOC value of battery cells is usually over zero when vehicle crashes occur during driving. In this investigation, the SOC dependent mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion battery cells was revealed based on the clay-like mechanical properties. A finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account. Then, the finite element model was validated through indentation tests with a rigid rod. For electrochemical behaviors, initial short circuit may be directly reflected by the start of voltage drop. During indentation tests, the voltage responses were measured to research the electrochemical failure behaviors of Lithium-ion battery cells with various SOC values. Experimental results indicate that the voltage would increase slightly at first, and the initial short circuit occurred simultaneously with the load reaching its peak value. 2. Experimental program on battery cells Commercial 18650 Lithium-ion cells were used in this study due to their ease of use in the current investigation, manufactured by SONY. WDW-100 10 T Universal Test Machine was used to perform the compressive test, see Fig. 1. Meanwhile, a safety shield made of polymethyl methacrylate was placed within the boundary of the testing machine to provide necessary protection against possible fire and even explosion. These cylindrical cells with various SOC values were experimented under compression between two flat steel plates at a quasi-static rate of 0.5 mm/min. Compressive experiment on the battery cell at each SOC value was repeated at least three times to ensure the accuracy of the measured data. These measured load-displacement curves are shown in Fig. 2. It is obvious that Lithium-ion battery cells will exhibit mechanical hardening mechanism with the increase in SOC values, as depicted in the Fig. 2. 130 WenWei Wang et al. / Energy Procedia 145 (2018) 128â€“132 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 (a) (b) (c) Fig. 1. (a) Cylindrical cell compressed between two flat steel plates, (b) FLUKE 2638A, and (c) Cylindrical cell compressed by rigid rod. 3. SOC dependent clay-like mechanical property of jellyroll As depicted in Ref. [6], the measured load-displacement curves could be used to obtain the constitutive model of the jellyroll and there is no need to repeat the derivation procedure. The constitutive model of the jellyroll is expressed in the form: ln ln pA Bï�³ ï�¥ï€½ ï€« (1) where ï�³ and pï�¥ are the plastic stress and plastic strain of the jellyroll, and A and B are constant, determined by the characteristics of the battery cells. Fig. 2. Measured Load-displacement curve, compressed between two flat plates. In Ref. [6], Lithium-ion battery cells used in compressive experiments are all discharged at a state of charge of almost 0% and it was found that changing thickness of the coating active particles will influence the mechanical properties of the jellyroll. When the battery cells at zero SOC are charged to various SOC value, the overall thickness of the active particle coatings will increase due to insertion and removal of Li-ions ( Liï€« ) into/from the host materials (active electrodes). When the battery cells are charged from zero SOC to a given SOC value, the amount of Liï€« inserted or removed from the active electrode materials during chemical reaction could be regarded 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 as constant. That is to say, the thickness increase of the active particle coatings is a constant value at a given SOC. Like the clay, jellyrollâ€™s ability to resist deformation increases certainly with certain thickness increase. Besides, the stress-strain of jellyroll shows a linear relationship on the ln ln pï�³ ï�¥ plane as described in Ref. [6]. Therefore, the SOC dependent constitutive model of the jellyroll could be built as: 0 0ln ln ( ) pA B D SOCï�³ ï�¥ï€½ ï€« ï€« (2) where 0A and 0B are parameters at the zero SOC, ( )D SOC presents the SOC function, depending on the SOC value. 4. Validation Fig. 3. (a) Voltage and Load vs. Displacement curves of 0.5 SOC cells, (b) Voltage and Load vs. Displacement curves of 0.6 SOC cells, and (c) cells after thermal runaway The cell voltage was measured in-situ by FLUKE 2638A during the indentation experiments, as shown in Fig. 1 (b) and (c). During compression, the voltage would increase slightly at first, around 0.5 mv, probably because a small amount of Li+ may insert to the graphite to change the electrode potential, as depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). The internal short circuit occurred simultaneously when the load reached its peak value. After the short circuit, the downtrend of voltage was similar to the load and a few seconds were needed before the voltage dropped to zero, as WenWei Wang et al. / Energy Procedia 145 (2018) 128â€“132 131 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 (a) (b) (c) Fig. 1. (a) Cylindrical cell compressed between two flat steel plates, (b) FLUKE 2638A, and (c) Cylindrical cell compressed by rigid rod. 3. SOC dependent clay-like mechanical property of jellyroll As depicted in Ref. [6], the measured load-displacement curves could be used to obtain the constitutive model of the jellyroll and there is no need to repeat the derivation procedure. The constitutive model of the jellyroll is expressed in the form: ln ln pA Bï�³ ï�¥ï€½ ï€« (1) where ï�³ and pï�¥ are the plastic stress and plastic strain of the jellyroll, and A and B are constant, determined by the characteristics of the battery cells. Fig. 2. Measured Load-displacement curve, compressed between two flat plates. In Ref. [6], Lithium-ion battery cells used in compressive experiments are all discharged at a state of charge of almost 0% and it was found that changing thickness of the coating active particles will influence the mechanical properties of the jellyroll. When the battery cells at zero SOC are charged to various SOC value, the overall thickness of the active particle coatings will increase due to insertion and removal of Li-ions ( Liï€« ) into/from the host materials (active electrodes). When the battery cells are charged from zero SOC to a given SOC value, the amount of Liï€« inserted or removed from the active electrode materials during chemical reaction could be regarded 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 as constant. That is to say, the thickness increase of the active particle coatings is a constant value at a given SOC. Like the clay, jellyrollâ€™s ability to resist deformation increases certainly with certain thickness increase. Besides, the stress-strain of jellyroll shows a linear relationship on the ln ln pï�³ ï�¥ plane as described in Ref. [6]. Therefore, the SOC dependent constitutive model of the jellyroll could be built as: 0 0ln ln ( ) pA B D SOCï�³ ï�¥ï€½ ï€« ï€« (2) where 0A and 0B are parameters at the zero SOC, ( )D SOC presents the SOC function, depending on the SOC value. 4. Validation Fig. 3. (a) Voltage and Load vs. Displacement curves of 0.5 SOC cells, (b) Voltage and Load vs. Displacement curves of 0.6 SOC cells, and (c) cells after thermal runaway The cell voltage was measured in-situ by FLUKE 2638A during the indentation experiments, as shown in Fig. 1 (b) and (c). During compression, the voltage would increase slightly at first, around 0.5 mv, probably because a small amount of Li+ may insert to the graphite to change the electrode potential, as depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). The internal short circuit occurred simultaneously when the load reached its peak value. After the short circuit, the downtrend of voltage was similar to the load and a few seconds were needed before the voltage dropped to zero, as 132 WenWei Wang et al. / Energy Procedia 145 (2018) 128â€“132 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA to validate the proposed approach. All the predicted load-displacement curves closely follow the corresponding experiments, as shown in Fig. 3. The peak force and its point of displacement are corresponding to the onset of internal short circuit. The good simulation results validated the SOC dependent clay-like mechanical property of the jellyroll. 5. Discussion and conclusion SOC dependent mechanical behaviors are extremely important in studying the crashworthiness of Lithium-ion battery cells, especially at the operation situation when electrochemical cycle occur and the SOC value is over zero. In this paper, several quasi-static compressive experiments on cylindrical Lithium-ion battery cells at various SOC values have been performed to reveal the exact and quantitative relationships between mechanical behavior and SOC status. The SOC dependent mechanical properties in compression were found clay-like through a hybrid analytical/experimental approach. These experiment results indicated that Lithium-ion battery cells would exhibited mechanical hardening mechanism with the increase in SOC values. An explicit finite element model for the Lithium-ion battery cells was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA to predict the SOC dependent mechanical response. Another indentation tests were performed on the Lithium-ion battery cells at various SOC values to validate it. Simulation results are in good agreement with the corresponding experiments at each loading scenario. This research may make it possible to carry out crush simulations of an entire battery pack at operation condition, and also provide important information for the safe design and evaluation of single battery cells and battery packs. Future research would focus on the electrical failure mechanism of Lithium-ion battery cells induced by mechanical deformation/stress and establish corresponding fracture model and criteria. Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge sincerely Prof. Rui Xiong and his Advanced Energy Storage and Application (AESA) Group at Beijing Institute of Technology for providing the battery experiment support. The authors would also like to acknowledge the financial support of the National key R&D Program of China (No. 2017YFB0103801). References [1] Yan S, Xiao X, Huang X, Li X, Qi Y. Unveiling the environment-dependent mechanical properties of porous polypropylene separators. Polymer 2014; 55: 6282-6292. [2] XW Zhang, Tomasz W. Characterization of plasticity and fracture of shell casing of lithium-ion cylindrical battery. J Power Sources 2015; 280: 47-56. [3] Sethuraman VA, Chon MJ, Shimshak M, Van Winkle N, Guduru PR. In situ measurement of biaxial modulus of Si anode for Li-ion batteries. Electrochem Commun 2010; 12: 1614-1617. [4] Mehdi G, Ilya A. Impact modeling of cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells: a heterogeneous approach. J Power Sources 2016; 328: 443-451. [5] Elham S, Emanuela B, Brandy D, Benjamin L. Microscale failure mechanisms leading to internal short circuit in Li-ion batteries under complex loading scenarios. J Power Sources 2016; 319: 56-65. [6] WW Wang, S Yang, C Lin. Clay-like mechanical properties for the jellyroll of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells. Appl Energy 2017; 196: 249-258. [7] Tao X, Du J, Sun YM, Zhou S, Xia Y, Huang H. Exploring the energy storage mechanism of high performance MnO2 electrochemical capacitor electrodes: an in situ atomic force microscopy study in aqueous electrolyte. Adv Funct Mater 2013; 23: 4745-4751. sert to the graphite to change the electrode potential, as depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). The internal short circuit occurred simultaneously when the load reached its peak value. After the short circuit, the downtrend of voltage was similar to the load and a few seconds were needed before the voltage dropped to zero, as 132 WenWei Wang et al. / Energy Procedia 145 (2018) 128â€“132 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 depicted in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA to validate the proposed approach. All the predicted load-displacement curves closely follow the corresponding experiments, as shown in Fig. 3. The peak force and its point of displacement are corresponding to the onset of internal short circuit. The good simulation results validated the SOC dependent clay-like mechanical property of the jellyroll. 5. Discussion and conclusion SOC dependent mechanical behaviors are extremely important in studying the crashworthiness of Lithium-ion battery cells, especially at the operation situation when electrochemical cycle occur and the SOC value is over zero. In this paper, several quasi-static compressive experiments on cylindrical Lithium-ion battery cells at various SOC values have been performed to reveal the exact and quantitative relationships between mechanical behavior and SOC status. The SOC dependent mechanical properties in compression were found clay-like through a hybrid analytical/experimental approach. These experiment results indicated that Lithium-ion battery cells would exhibited mechanical hardening mechanism with the increase in SOC values. An explicit finite element model for the Lithium-ion battery cells was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA to predict the SOC dependent mechanical response. Another indentation tests were performed on the Lithium-ion battery cells at various SOC values to validate it. Simulation results are in good agreement with the corresponding experiments at each loading scenario. This research may make it possible to carry out crush simulations of an entire battery pack at operation condition, and also provide important information for the safe design and evaluation of single battery cells and battery packs. Future research would focus on the electrical failure mechanism of Lithium-ion battery cells induced by mechanical deformation/stress and establish corresponding fracture model and criteria. Acknowledgements The authors would like to acknowledge sincerely Prof. Rui Xiong and his Advanced Energy Storage and Application (AESA) Group at Beijing Institute of Technology for providing the battery experiment support. The authors would also like to acknowledge the financial support of the National key R&D Program of China (No. 2017YFB0103801). References [1] Yan S, Xiao X, Huang X, Li X, Qi Y. Unveiling the environment-dependent mechanical properties of porous polypropylene separators. Polymer 2014; 55: 6282-6292. [2] XW Zhang, Tomasz W. Characterization of plasticity and fracture of shell casing of lithium-ion cylindrical battery. J Power Sour

head.ce:title: Mechanical and electrical response of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells at various State of Charge

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Electric Vehicles in Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China, 100081

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent evidence has shown that the mechanical properties of lithium-ion battery will change with the change of State of Charge (SOC) value. In this investigation, several quasi-static mechanical tests on 18650 battery cells with various SOC values were performed to reveal the SOC dependent mechanical behaviors and the voltage response of Lithium-ion battery. Experimental results indicate that the ability of Lithium-ion battery to resist deformation will increase with SOC value increasing. An explicit finite element model was established in HyperWorks/LS-DYNA with taking SOC effect into account.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unveiling the environment-dependent mechanical properties of porous polypropylene separators

Impact modeling of cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells: a heterogeneous approach

Clay-like mechanical properties for the jellyroll of cylindrical Lithium-ion cells
Exploring the energy storage mechanism of high performance MnO2 electrochemical capacitor electrodes: an in situ atomic force microscopy study in aqueous electrolyte
